title: Dog Tail Chasing Non-Stop: Causes, Urgency, and Care Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog chasing their tail constantly? Learn about potential medical and behavioral causes, and determine if you need an urgent vet visit.
content_html:
What is Non-Stop Tail Chasing?

While occasional tail chasing can be normal play, non-stop or compulsive chasing is usually a sign of an underlying issue. This behavior may be physical, stemming from skin allergies, impacted anal glands, or spinal discomfort. Alternatively, it can be a behavioral condition known as Canine Compulsive Disorder (CCD), which is often triggered by stress, boredom, or anxiety.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

The urgency level for this behavior is Medium. While it is rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, it requires a veterinary appointment as soon as possible. If left untreated, dogs can cause significant trauma to their tails through biting and chewing, leading to infections or permanent nerve damage. If your dog is currently bleeding or cannot be distracted from the behavior, seek care within 24 hours.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Capturing a video of the chasing behavior is one of the most helpful tools for your veterinarian. Because dogs often act differently in a clinic setting, a video shows the vet the intensity and frequency of the episodes at home. Additionally, close-up photos of the tail and the area around the base of the tail can help the vet identify signs of parasites, inflammation, or injury without causing further stress to your pet.

faqs.question: Could my dog have a flea allergy?
faqs.answer: Yes, flea allergy dermatitis often causes intense itching at the base of the tail, which may lead to a dog spinning or chasing to reach the source of the irritation.

faqs.question: Is tail chasing a sign of boredom?
faqs.answer: It can be. High-energy breeds may develop repetitive behaviors if they do not receive enough mental and physical stimulation throughout the day.

faqs.question: Are certain breeds more prone to tail chasing?
faqs.answer: Yes, breeds like Bull Terriers and German Shepherds are genetically more predisposed to developing compulsive spinning or tail-chasing behaviors.
